title: Rules of Self
description: Rules of the Self focuses on the internal patterns that shape how we create. Instead of following an external instruction, this rule asks you to pay attention to your own instincts, habits, and tendencies as you work. What choices do you make automatically? What do you avoid? What feels “right” without explanation? These decisions often happen without thinking, but they form a kind of personal rule system that guides the entire process. By noticing these patterns in real time, the act of making becomes more reflective. The goal isn’t to fix or change anything, but to recognize how much of the work is influenced by something internal and often invisible. Rules of the Self turns the focus inward, treating the creative process as a way of observing not just what you make, but how you make it.

rules.rule: Put something down, no matter how bad
rules.author: Noah Ortiz
rules.description: Get a first version onto the page before refining it. Momentum matters more than polish at the beginning.
rules.process: I forced myself to make the first marks without evaluating them so the page could become active immediately.

rules.rule: Make small things, smaller pieces and projects can inspire and change bigger ones without all the pressure.
rules.author: Bryce Walsh
rules.description: Shrink the scale of the work so it feels lighter and more flexible while still influencing the larger direction.
rules.process: I made smaller studies first and let them guide the bigger piece without overloading it with pressure.

rules.rule: FUCK AROUND N' FIND OUT
rules.author: Maddie Hickey
rules.description: Experiment directly and accept that the process may reveal its own limits through action.
rules.process: I tested a few reckless variations to see what would happen when the work was pushed beyond caution.

rules.rule: Design with empathy.
rules.author: Jazmin Corrujedo
rules.description: Let the work consider the people who will encounter it and the needs they bring with them.
rules.process: I shaped the piece around how someone else might experience it rather than only how I wanted it to look.

rules.rule: Don't be afraid to start over
rules.author: Molly Vanderbogart
rules.description: Resetting the work is not failure; it can be the clearest way to move forward.
rules.process: I abandoned an early direction and began again once it became clear that a cleaner restart would serve the piece better.

rules.rule: Have a vision in your mind (picture your ideas clearly).
rules.author: Hayley Rocker
rules.description: Hold the idea clearly before you begin so the work can stay oriented to an internal image.
rules.process: I kept the intended result in mind throughout the process and used that image to guide decisions.

rules.rule: Write out the jumbled mess of ideas that pop into your brain
rules.author: Daneisha Espinosa
rules.description: Capture the raw, unruly thoughts first so they can be sorted or refined later.
rules.process: I wrote down every fragment that came to mind before trying to organize anything into a structure.

rules.rule: Create as much as you can before judging it as good or bad.
rules.author: Ginger Defino
rules.description: Generate work first and postpone evaluation so the act of making can continue without interruption.
rules.process: I kept producing variations before critiquing any of them, which helped the work develop without early collapse.

rules.rule: Don’t focus in on one idea right at the start, explore many options and ideations
rules.author: Christian Vitale
rules.description: Begin broadly and let multiple possibilities exist before narrowing the direction.
rules.process: I sketched several different directions in parallel and only later chose the one worth developing.

rules.rule: Always ask questions
rules.author: Elysia Shannon
rules.description: Use questioning as a method for pushing the work into more thoughtful territory.
rules.process: I treated every step as something to interrogate so the piece stayed open instead of fixed.

rules.rule: Don’t think, just do.
rules.author: Madison Tamburrini
rules.description: Move into action before overanalyzing the outcome.
rules.process: I started making immediately and let the work reveal itself through iteration instead of hesitation.

rules.rule: Make sure to take a step back / a breather from your work to look at it with fresh eyes.
rules.author: Faith Wilson
rules.description: Pause long enough to return with perspective and evaluate the work anew.
rules.process: I stepped away from the piece and came back later to see details that were invisible while I was too close to it.

rules.rule: Sit on a design to see how I feel on it
rules.author: Noah Ortiz
rules.description: Leave the design alone for a while so your reaction to it can settle naturally.
rules.process: I let the piece sit untouched before returning to it, which made my response to it much clearer.

rules.rule: Just go for it, work as if you're under pressure.
rules.author: Maddie Hickey
rules.description: Treat the work like a live moment so urgency can sharpen the choices.
rules.process: I imposed a pressure-filled pace on myself and used it to keep the piece moving forward.

rules.rule: Does it make some people turn heads?
rules.author: Jazmin Corrujedo
rules.description: Consider whether the work has enough presence to catch attention.
rules.process: I checked whether the piece had a strong enough visual pull to make someone stop and look.

rules.rule: Will it please other people
rules.author: Daneisha Espinosa
rules.description: Think about the response the work might provoke in others.
rules.process: I asked whether the piece would communicate clearly beyond my own perspective.

rules.rule: Don’t force creativity, when it’s not working take a step back and see the bigger picture
rules.author: Christian Vitale
rules.description: Avoid pushing too hard and allow distance to reveal the larger structure.
rules.process: I paused when the work stalled and returned to the broader composition rather than forcing a solution.

rules.rule: It has to be perfect.
rules.author: Madison Tamburrini
rules.description: Hold the work to a strict standard of precision and completion.
rules.process: I kept refining the piece until it met the exacting level I had set for it.

rules.rule: I can't move onto another idea until this one is 'perfect'
rules.author: Faith Wilson
rules.description: Stay with one direction until it feels fully resolved before switching to the next.
rules.process: I refused to jump ahead and kept returning to the same idea until it felt finished.

rules.rule: I likely think this design is a lot worse than it really is
rules.author: Nikki Hirschkind
rules.description: Trust that your self-critique may be harsher than the work deserves.
rules.process: I evaluated the design with the assumption that my first impression might be too severe.

rules.rule: Doodle like you've never doodled before.
rules.author: Madison Tamburrini
rules.description: Treat doodling as an active space for exploration rather than a throwaway gesture.
rules.process: I let the marks expand freely and used doodling as a way to discover unexpected forms.

rules.rule: It's good you're not the best designer in the room, how would you grow if you were?
rules.author: Sarah Friedlander
rules.description: Stay in a position where growth is still possible and improvement still matters.
rules.process: I used the feeling of being slightly outpaced as a prompt to learn instead of compare.

rules.rule: Perfection is dull, and flaws are honest.
rules.author: Danielle Fiumara
rules.description: Let imperfections remain visible because they carry more truth than polished sameness.
rules.process: I preserved the irregularities so the work could retain the evidence of how it was made.

rules.rule: It's ok to ask for help when you are stuck
rules.author: Nicky Thomas
rules.description: Treat asking for help as part of the process rather than as a failure.
rules.process: I reached out when I got stuck so the work could continue moving instead of freezing in place.

rules.rule: Have fun making it.
rules.author: Clark Molfetta
rules.description: Keep the process playful so the making itself remains alive.
rules.process: I tried to keep the tone light while I worked, which changed how openly I approached each decision.

rules.rule: Don't stop yourself from creating because you're scared it won't turn out 'right'. You need to create.
rules.author: Emma Ferenczy
rules.description: Create despite uncertainty instead of letting fear prevent the work from starting.
rules.process: I made the piece without waiting for confidence to arrive, because the act of creating had to come first.

rules.rule: A sketch is a sketch, don’t be so delicate when sketching; it’s not the final product... feel free to mess it up.
rules.author: Bryce Walsh
rules.description: Treat sketches as working material and allow them to be rough, messy, and unfinished.
rules.process: I drew loosely and intentionally let the sketch stay unstable so it could remain open to change.

rules.rule: Create something different/unique
rules.author: Ginger Defino
rules.description: Aim for difference so the outcome can break from repetition.
rules.process: I pushed the work toward a more singular result instead of repeating familiar solutions.
